article_meta.title: News Law & the Courts Black Lives Matter Director Spent Millions in Donations on Homes, Shopping, Vacations, Indictment

transcript.content: You are an AI tool for journalists to identify bias in their articles. Analyze the article and return biased phrases, their bias types, and explanations. IMPORTANT ignore everything that quotes other people, only use what author is saying.CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Output ONLY a valid JSON array. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences. If no bias is found, output an empty array: [] Format:[{"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}, {"biasType": "Type of media bias", "biasedText": "Example from the article", "explanation": "explanation"}] Example:[{"biasType": "Mind reading", "biasedText": "In a statement, Pillen characterized the NSEA's petition as an attack on children seeking the best education.", "explanation": "Assumes Pillen's view reflects the NSEA's intentions."}]

Types of media bias:- Spin: Vague or sensational language, including substituting an interpretive verb for a neutral one. Example: Writing "Senator X gloated over the bill's passage" rather than "said" or "responded" — the word 'gloated' implies smug pleasure without evidence, in the author's own voice.- Unsubstantiated claims: Claims without evidence.- Opinion Statements Presented as Fact: Subjective judgments presented as objective truth, without attribution. Example: A reporter writes in their own voice "The tax cuts failed to stimulate economic growth" — whether a policy "failed" is a contested  causal judgment that belongs in analysis, not unattributed news copy. - Sensationalism and Emotionalism: Information presented to shock.- Mudslinging/Ad Hominem: Unfair insults or attacks on character.- Mind reading: Assuming knowledge of others' thoughts.- Slant: Telling only part of the story present in the article — where information exists in the article but is structurally de-emphasized.   Example: An article reports a protester was injured prominently in the opening paragraphs, but mentions that three officers were also treated for injuries only in a single sentence near the end, attributed to a police statement. Both facts appear, but unequal prominence shapes the reader's impression. - Bias by Omission: Omitting information that would support an alternative viewpoint entirely — where the alternative perspective is absent from the article, not merely de-emphasized.   Example: An article on immigration enforcement quotes only affected families and advocacy organizations. No law enforcement perspective, border security rationale, or policy justification appears anywhere in the piece — the alternative viewpoint does not exist in the article at all. - Subjective Qualifying Adjectives: Bias revealed through adjectives.- Word Choice: Politically loaded words.- Negativity Bias: Emphasizing negative news.- Elite vs. Populist Bias: Deferring to prestigious institutions vs. dissenting perspectives. Exclude quotes from analysis. Be cautious and humble in your analysis. Present fewer, confident examples rather than more uncertain ones. CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION RULE: When the author reproduces a quoted source's statement at length (e.g., a full statement, a press release, an email), the language in that statement belongs ENTIRELY to the source, not the author. Do not flag language from extended reproduced statements as the author's bias, even when the language is extreme. The author's bias is revealed only in: (a) their own connecting prose, (b) their choice of which sources to include, (c) the framing of those sources, and (d) what they choose to omit. Analyze this article:

---
HEADLINE: News Law & the Courts Black Lives Matter Director Spent Millions in Donations on Homes, Shopping, Vacations, Indictment 
SOURCE: Forgotten Fact Checks (National Review)

ARTICLE:

News Law & the Courts Black Lives Matter Director Spent Millions in Donations on Homes, Shopping, Vacations, Indictment Alleges By Kamden Mulder December 12, 2025 9:42 AM Share on Facebook Share on Twitter Share on Flipboard Share on WhatsApp Email this article Rev. Sheri Dickerson rejoices after Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt granted clemency for Julius Jones, who was sentenced to death for the 1999 fatal shooting of Paul Howell, in McAlester, Okla., November 18, 2021. (Nick Oxford/Reuters) Oklahoma City Black Lives Matter Executive Director Tashella Sheri Amore Dickerson has been charged with 20 counts of wire fraud and five counts of money laundering after allegedly spending millions in donations on personal indulgences. Dickerson took over as the director of Black Lives Matter OKC (BLMOCK) in 2016 and since 2020 has raised more than $5.6 million for what donors believed was a national bail fund. The bail fund was also supplemented by grants through the Community Justice Exchange, Massachusetts Bail Fund, and Minnesota Freedom Fund. The indictment alleges that from June 2020 to October 2025, Dickerson used at least $3.15 million in bail fund donations and grant money to supplement her lifestyle. Dickerson allegedly embezzled the funds to pay for personal shopping sprees, $50,000 in food and grocery delivery, trips to Jamaica and the Dominican Republic, as well as a personal vehicle and six Oklahoma City properties registered in her name. The indictment explains that Dickerson allegedly used interstate wire communications to send false reports to Alliance for Global Justice, a fiscal sponsor to BLMOCK, which only permitted the group to use its funds in ways compliant with its 501(c)3 nonprofit status. Dickerson, however, did not disclose how she was allegedly using the funds for personal gain. If convicted, Dickerson faces up to 20 years in federal prison and a $250,000 fine per count of wire fraud. For each count of money laundering, she faces ten years in prison and a fine of up to $250,000, or twice the amount of criminally derived property. Donations began pouring into Black Lives Matter and affiliated racial justice organizations in 2020 following the murder of George Floyd, setting off a spending spree among leaders who suddenly had access to millions of dollars with very little oversight. According to the group's 2020-2022 tax filings, only one third of the organization's $90 million in revenue was used to fund the charity. Patrisse Cullors who ran the Black Lives Matter Global Network Foundation, the movement's umbrella organization, spent millions on job contracts for friends and family members as well as several multi-million dollar homes. Cullors resigned in 2021 in response to backlash from BLM chapters across the country though she was never charged criminally and has denied any wrongdoing. BLM activist Monica Cannon-Grant pled guilty in September to multiple counts of wire fraud after diverting $185,000 in donations to her nonprofit, Violence in Boston, to pay for rent, shopping sprees, hotels, car rentals, auto repairs, meal deliveries, and a summer vacation trip to Maryland. The Justice Department launched an investigation into BLM and similar activist groups in October. Kamden Mulder is a William F. Buckley Jr. Fellow in Political Journalism. She is a graduate of Hillsdale College. More in Law & the Courts The Mind-Boggling Saga of Kilmar Abrego Garcia The Mind-Boggling Saga of Kilmar Abrego Garcia Officials Who Abuse Their Power Should Not Get a Free Pass Officials Who Abuse Their Power Should Not Get a Free Pass 'Narco-Terrorism' Is a Legally Meaningless Term 'Narco-Terrorism' Is a Legally Meaningless Term There Is No Such Thing as an Independent Agency There Is No Such Thing as an Independent Agency
---

parsed_output.biasedText: Donations began pouring into Black Lives Matter and affiliated racial justice organizations in 2020 following the murder of George Floyd, setting off a spending spree among leaders who suddenly had access to millions of dollars with very little oversight.
parsed_output.explanation: The phrase 'setting off a spending spree' characterizes the leaders' financial management in strongly negative terms in the author's own voice, framing all subsequent spending as excessive and frivolous rather than neutrally reporting the increase in donations and subsequent expenditures. This interpretive characterization colors the reader's perception before presenting specific allegations.

The author's use of 'personal indulgences' in the opening paragraph is a loaded characterization that conveys excessive, self-gratifying behavior. A neutral alternative would be 'personal expenses' or simply stating what the indictment alleges without the judgmental framing.

parsed_output.biasedText: According to the group's 2020-2022 tax filings, only one third of the organization's $90 million in revenue was used to fund the charity.
parsed_output.explanation: This statement is presented without context about what portion of nonprofit revenue typically goes to direct charitable activities versus operational costs, salaries, and infrastructure. The word 'only' implies wrongdoing, but the article provides no benchmark for comparison, leaving readers to assume this is automatically problematic.
